283	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
284	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
285	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
286	% assertion."
287	\begin{proof}
288	  \leanok
289	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
290	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
291	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
292	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
293	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
294	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
295	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
296	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
297	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
298	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
299	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
300	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
301	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
302	  \medskip
303	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
304	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
305	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
306	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
307	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
308	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
309	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
310	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
311	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
312	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
313	  proven.
314	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
315	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
316	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
317	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
318	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
319	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
320	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
321	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
322	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
323	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
324	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
325	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).
326	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
327	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
328	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
329	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
330	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)
331	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
332	  stack is: for each closed point \(y \in V\) one has \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\) (alg-closed, finite type);
333	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
334	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)
335	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
336	  extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring
337	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so \(f\) and
338	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
339	  locally-of-finite-type \(U\) (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
340	  locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
341	  hypothesis \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)), so the per-slice
342	  agreement globalises to all of \(U\) via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
343	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
344	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
345	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
346	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
347	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
348	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
349	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
350	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
351	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
352	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
353	\end{proof}
354	\begin{lemma}
355	\leanok

360	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
361	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
362	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
363	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
364	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
365	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
366	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
367	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
368	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
369	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
370	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
371	  % is retired.
372	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
373	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
374	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
375	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
376	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
377	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
378	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
379	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
380	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
381	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
382	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
383	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
384	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
385	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
386	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
387	  endomorphism. Suppose:
388	  \begin{itemize}
389	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
390	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
391	      its points);
392	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
393	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
394	  \end{itemize}
395	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
396	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
397	\end{lemma}
398	\begin{proof}
399	  \leanok
400	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
401	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
402	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
403	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
404	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
405	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
406	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
407	  \medskip
408	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
409	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
410	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
411	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
412	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
413	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
414	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
415	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
416	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
417	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
418	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
419	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
420	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
421	  \medskip
422	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
423	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
424	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
425	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
426	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
427	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
428	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
429	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
430	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
431	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
432	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
433	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
434	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
435	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
436	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
437	\end{proof}
438	\begin{lemma}\leanok
439	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
440	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
441	  
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
443	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
444	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
445	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
446	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
447	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
448	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
449	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
450	\end{lemma}
451	\begin{proof}
452	\leanok
453	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
454	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
455	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
456	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
457	  dominant probe.
458	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
459	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
461	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
462	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
463	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
464	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
465	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
466	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
467	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
468	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
469	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
470	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
471	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
472	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
473	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
474	\end{proof}
475	\begin{lemma}
476	\leanok
477	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
478	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
480	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
481	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
482	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
483	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
484	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
485	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
486	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
487	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
488	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
489	  \medskip
490	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
491	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
492	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
494	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
495	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
496	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
497	  on global sections.
498	\end{lemma}
499	\begin{proof}
500	\leanok
501	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
502	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
503	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
504	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
505	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
506	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
507	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
508	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
509	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
510	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
511	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
512	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
513	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
514	  quoted from Mumford above.
515	\end{proof}
516	\begin{lemma}
517	\leanok
518	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
519	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
520	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
521	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
522	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
523	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
524	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
525	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
526	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
527	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
528	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
529	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
530	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).
531	  Then \(X\) is integral.
532	\end{lemma}
533	\begin{proof}
534	\leanok
535	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
536	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
537	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
538	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
539	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
540	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
541	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
542	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
543	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
544	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
545	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
546	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
547	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
548	\end{proof}

597	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-\(0\) base case. The two
598	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
599	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
600	G_m\(-scaling action \)\sigma_\times\( on \)\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
601	the base case ``every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
602	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one axis of the
603	decomposition, giving \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense \(\mathbb G_m\). The
604	classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive homomorphism step
605	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
606	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
607	\emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
608	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
609	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\( is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete \)\mathbb
610	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
611	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
612	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
613	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
614	path. The genus-\(0\) output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
615	(here: regular) map \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant''.

677	  \(f \circ p + g \circ q\) is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
678	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as \(h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)\) in the group
679	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)\), and does \emph{not} require \(A\) commutative; commutativity of

690	  \leanok
691	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
692	  Define \(f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}\) and \(g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}\), identifying
693	  \(V \times \{w_0\} \cong V\) and \(\{v_0\} \times W \cong W\); on points \(f(v) = h(v, w_0)\) and
694	  \(g(w) = h(v_0, w)\). Form the difference
695	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
696	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
697	  (the subtraction is in the group \(A\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is the composite of
698	  \((h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))\) with the group law of \(A\)). Then \(\varphi\) vanishes on both
699	  axes: \(\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)\) (direct computation, using
700	  \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\)). In particular \(\varphi\) collapses the complete axis \(V \times \{w_0\}\) to
701	  the single point \(0 \in A\), so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with \(V\)
702	  complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically irreducible) \(\varphi\) factors through the projection
703	  \(q\); but it also vanishes on \(\{v_0\} \times W\), which is a section of \(q\), so the factoring
704	  morphism is identically \(0\), i.e.\ \(\varphi \equiv 0\). Hence \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\).
705	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers \(f\) and \(g\) as the
706	  axis-restrictions of \(h\).
707	\end{proof}

822	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory (\(\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1\), prime divisors,
823	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
824	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
825	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) separately, that the
826	  morphism \(\Spec k(V) \to A\) extends over \(\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}\) using only that \(A\) is
827	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at \(Z\) without
828	  globally building \(\mathrm{div}(f)\)) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
829	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
830	  An earlier draft routed the genus-\(0\) base case through this extension, by collapsing the
831	  additive-defect map \(\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)\) on the \emph{affine} surface
832	  \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and then extending \(\psi\) over the complete surface
833	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
834	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
835	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
836	  \emph{total} translation action \(\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1\)
837	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): \(h := \sigma \fatsemi f\) is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
838	  \times \mathbb G_a\( (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is \)\mathbb P^1$
839	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
840	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-\(1\) indeterminacy
841	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
842	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
843	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
844	  them. The genus-\(0\) close no longer depends on any of them.
845	\end{remark}
846	\section{The genus-\(0\) base objects and the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling action (the primary route)}
847	\begin{definition}
848	[Concrete genus-\(0\) base objects: \(\mathbb P^1\), \(\mathbb G_a\), \(\mathbb G_m\) over \(\bar k\)]
849	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar}
851	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of standard objects; the mathematics is textbook (Hartshorne
852	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on \(\mathbb A^1\) are elementary). No external
853	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
854	  Work over \(\Spec \bar k\) with \(\bar k\) algebraically closed. The primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
855	  route (and its \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
856	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\):
857	  \begin{itemize}
858	    \item \(\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), the projective line --- a smooth proper
859	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) (Mathlib: projective space, or \(\mathrm{Proj}\)
860	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
861	      the distinguished \(\bar k\)-points \(0\), \(1\), \(\infty\) and the two standard affine charts
862	      \(\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\) (coordinate \(x\)) and
863	      \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}\) (coordinate \(u = 1/x\)).
864	    \item \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}\), the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
865	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,\(\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)\) with the \(\mathtt{GrpObj}\)
866	      additive structure \((x, y) \mapsto x + y\), identity \(0\); \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
867	      As a scheme \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\), and the open
868	      immersion \(\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1\) has dense (cofinite) image.
869	    \item \(\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}\), the
870	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law \((x, y) \mapsto xy\), identity \(1\); \lean name
871	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into \(\mathbb P^1\) also has dense image.
872	  \end{itemize}
873	  All three are of finite type over \(\bar k\); \(\mathbb P^1\) is proper, while \(\mathbb G_a\) and
874	  \(\mathbb G_m\) are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-\(0\)'' geometric inputs
875	  the base case needs.
876	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
54	
55	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
56	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
58	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
59	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
60	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
61	
62	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
63	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
64	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
65	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
66	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
67	\end{theorem}
68	
69	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
70	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
71	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
72	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
73	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
76	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
77	\begin{proof}
78	  
79	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
80	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
81	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
82	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
83	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
84	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
85	
86	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
87	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
88	  \begin{enumerate}
89	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
90	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
91	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
92	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
93	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
94	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
95	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
96	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
97	  \end{enumerate}
98	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
99	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
100	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
101	\end{proof}
102	
103	\begin{remark}
104	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
105	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
106	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
107	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
108	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
109	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
110	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
111	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
112	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
113	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
114	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
115	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
116	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
118	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
119	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
120	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
121	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
122	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
123	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
124	
125	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
126	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
127	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
128	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
129	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
130	\end{remark}
131	
132	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
133	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
134	
135	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
136	\begin{itemize}
137	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
138	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
139	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
140	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
141	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
142	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
143	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
144	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
145	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
146	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
147	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
148	    has a characteristic restriction.
149	\end{itemize}
150	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
151	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
152	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
153	
154	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
155	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
156	
157	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
158	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
159	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
160	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
161	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
162	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
163	defined by
164	\[
165	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
166	\]
167	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
168	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
170	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
171	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
172	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
173	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
174	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
175	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
176	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
177	
178	\section{Lean encoding}
179	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
180	
181	The Lean target file is the new
182	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
183	this chapter targets is the theorem
184	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
185	notation):
186	\[
187	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
188	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
189	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
190	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
191	\]
192	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
193	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
195	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
196	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
197	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
198	
199	The Mathlib namespace target is
200	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
201	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
202	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
203	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
204	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
205	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
206	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
207	
208	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
209	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
210	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
211	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
212	
213	\section{Out of scope}
214	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
215	
216	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
217	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
218	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
219	\begin{itemize}
220	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
221	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
222	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
223	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
224	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
225	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
226	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
227	    (Milne~\(\equiv\) Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
228	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
229	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
230	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
231	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
232	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
233	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
234	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
235	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) is constant'',
236	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
237	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
238	    current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case
239	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
240	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
241	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
242	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
243	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
244	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
245	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
246	    \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to \(C^{(g)} \to A\).
247	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
248	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
249	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
250	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
251	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
252	\end{itemize}

32:\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
40:\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.

72:  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (Lean: \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}; in the use case, the algebraic closure of a base field \(k\), with descent of the conclusion to \(k\) handled downstream in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). These two hypotheses match the theorem's existing docstring and are required by the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}, \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}); see \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot". Let \(C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\) (i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\)). Let \(A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta_A \in A(\bar k)\). Let

2088:    lem:constants_integral_over_base_field,
2146:  The proof proceeds in five steps. Steps 1--2 establish that the chart-level universal K\"ahler derivation \(\mathrm dB \colon R \to \Omega_{R/k}\) satisfies \(\mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b)) = 0\) for every \(b \in B\), locally on a single affine chart \(V\), from the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\) and the standard-smooth presentation of \(\Omega_{A/k}\) on \(W\). Step 3 promotes this chart-local kernel-vanishing to global section-vanishing on \(C\) via the chart-\v Cech Mayer--Vietoris layer. Steps 4--5 extract the conclusion \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)\) via the ring-side helper (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}) and the integrally-closed-constants helper (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}).
2193:  \emph{Step 5: integrally-closed-constants closure via \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible over \(k\), the global sections \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\) are integral over \(k\) and (by the smooth-proper-geometrically-irreducible fact) coincide with \(\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C))\); the integrally-closed-constants helper packages this fact. The chart-restriction map \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) \hookrightarrow R\) (canonical sheaf restriction from \(C\) to \(V\), injective by sheaf separatedness on a reduced source) ensures that the image of \(k\) inside \(R\) also exhausts the integral closure of \(k\) in \(R\). Combining with Step 4 yields the conclusion \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)\).
2197:  \emph{Mathlib status.} The chart-side standard-smooth presentation: \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} are [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}. The K\"ahler-derivation functoriality \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map} / \texttt{relativeDifferentials'\_map\_d} is [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic} / \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf}. The chart-\v Cech kernel-equals-global-sections identification of Step 3 is supplied by the chart-algebra (\(\alpha\)) helper \cref{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product} plus the abstract affine-cover Mayer--Vietoris sequence \cref{thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact} (\cref{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}) instantiated on the 2-chart cover of Step 3.aux; the 2-chart cover construction itself consumes the \texttt{AffineCover} + \texttt{IsAffineOpen} + \texttt{LocalizationAway} API of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} + \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Away.Basic} (cf.\ Stacks Tag~0F8L for the construction). The two project-internal helpers \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} and \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} discharge Steps 4 and 5 respectively.

2215:  This sub-claim is the (S3.sep.1) gateway substep of path (b) of the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}: a smart-proof reduction of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k\) to the conjunction of separability and pure inseparability, where separability is delivered via geometric reducedness \(\Rightarrow\) separable on a finite field extension (\cref{lem:S3_sep_2_geom_reduced_finite_field_ext_is_separable}).
2226:  Smoothness of \(X / \Spec k\) implies that for any field extension \(K / k\) the base change \(X_K\) is reduced (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) formally smooth \(\Rightarrow\) geometrically reduced fibres; Stacks Tags~\textbf{035U} + \textbf{04QM}). The global-section ring \(\Gamma(X_K, \mathcal O_{X_K})\) injects into the stalks of \(X_K\) at its points (sheaf separatedness on a reduced source), each of which is reduced; hence \(\Gamma(X_K, \mathcal O_{X_K})\) is itself reduced as a \(K\)-algebra. By the flat-base-change identification \cref{lem:S3_pi_1_Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper} (whose content is the same as path (a) step~(e) of the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, just re-packaged as a single named lemma), \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k K \cong \Gamma(X_K, \mathcal O_{X_K})\) is reduced for every field extension \(K / k\). That is, \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) is geometrically reduced over \(k\). Estimated build: \(\sim 80\)--\(150\) LOC.

2257:  % path (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} no longer uses
2300:  % \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} no longer uses it.
2310:  This sub-claim is the (S3.pi.1) load-bearing substep of path (b) of the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}. Its content is the same content as path (a) step~(e), packaged as a single named lemma request rather than as a 7-step ad-hoc chain scattered across \(\Gamma_{\bar k}\)-cohomology infrastructure.
2337:  \emph{Honesty note.} The content of this lemma is the SAME content as path~(a) step~(e) of the 7-step chain in the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, not bypassed by the smart-proof path~(b). Path~(b)'s genuine advantage is that it re-packages the flat-base-change content as ONE named Mathlib lemma request, rather than as a 7-step ad-hoc chain scattered across \(\Gamma_{\bar k}\)-cohomology infrastructure.
2357:  \label{lem:S3_pi_2_isPurelyInseparable_of_unique_minPrime_baseChange}
2362:  % path (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} no longer uses
2390:  Equivalently: \(\Spec(F \otimes_k \bar k)\) is a single closed point. The standard criterion (Stacks Tag~\textbf{030K}: a finite field extension \(F / k\) is purely inseparable iff \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a local ring) immediately yields pure inseparability of \(F / k\). The dependence on \cref{lem:S3_pi_1_Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper} is at the consumer level: the unique-minimal-prime hypothesis on \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is supplied in the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} via \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k \cong \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})\) for \(X\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible (geometric irreducibility makes \(X_{\bar k}\) irreducible, so the right-hand side has a unique minimal prime). Estimated build: \(\sim 50\)--\(100\) LOC.
2422:  \label{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2423:  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.constants_integral_over_base_field}
2500:  \uses{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2522:  By \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} the global-section ring \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) coincides with the image of the canonical \(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\); in particular \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) is a copy of \(k\) inside \(X\)'s global structure sheaf.
2526:  For the integral-closure property: suppose \(b \in B\) is integral over \(\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)\), i.e.\ satisfies \(b^{n} + a_{n-1} b^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0 = 0\) in \(B\) with each \(a_i \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)\). Smoothness plus geometric irreducibility of \(X\) over \(k\) gives \(X\) integral (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) reduced; geometrically irreducible \(\Rightarrow\) irreducible), so the affine chart \(V = \Spec B\) embeds into the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) via the canonical generic-point localisation (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField}; an open immersion of integral schemes induces an injection on rings into \(K(X)\)). The same monic relation holds in \(K(X)\), with coefficients \(a_i \in k \subseteq K(X)\), so \(b\) is integral over \(k\) inside \(K(X)\). The integral closure of \(k\) in \(K(X)\) is the field of constants of \(X\), which equals \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) for \(X\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible over \(k\) (a standard fact, Stacks Tag~0BUG; the integral elements over \(k\) in \(K(X)\) correspond to regular functions on \(X\), which by properness are global sections, which by \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} are exactly \(k\)). Hence \(b \in k\) inside \(K(X)\), and pulling back along \(\rho_V\) exhibits \(b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)\).
2528:  Estimated build: \(\sim 80\)--\(150\) LOC. The dominant cost is the chart-to-function-field embedding \(B \hookrightarrow K(X)\) (an open-immersion-induced localisation idiom; chase via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appLE} on the generic point) and the "integral closure of \(k\) in \(K(X)\) equals \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\)" identification (a thin wrapper around the constants-vanishing of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} plus the standard normality-of-smooth-schemes fact).
2530:  \emph{Mathlib status.} The chart-of-reduced-source injectivity for \(\rho_V\) is [verified] via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsOpenImmersion}-namespace lemmas on the structure sheaf. The function-field embedding \(B \hookrightarrow K(X)\) for \(B\) an affine chart of an integral scheme is [verified in idiom] in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField}. The "integral closure of \(k\) in \(K(X)\) equals constants" identification for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme is the standard Stacks Tag~0BUG; in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} it is [gap]-fill via a thin in-tree wrapper that chains \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} with the normality-of-smooth-\(k\)-schemes instance (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.normal}).
2595:      \item \emph{Closer} (\({\sim}10\) LOC, verified): from ``\(b\) algebraic over \(k\)'' conclude \(b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,K)\). The simple extension \(k(b)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\) (\texttt{IntermediateField.adjoin.finiteDimensional}, applied to the integrality witnessed by \(\mathtt{IsAlgebraic}\,k\,b\)), hence integral over \(k\) (\texttt{Algebra.IsIntegral.of\_finite}); since \(k\) is algebraically closed, \(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,k(b)\) is bijective (\texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, \texttt{Mathlib.FieldTheory.IsAlgClosed.Basic}). The generator of \(k(b)\) --- and therefore \(b\) itself inside \(K\) --- thus lies in \(\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,K)\). This is the \emph{same} Mathlib lemma the project already invokes in the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}.

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/DualInverse.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/PresheafInternalHom.lean
8	
9	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
10	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
11	
12	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
13	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
14	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
15	\[
16	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
17	  \qquad
18	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
19	\]
20	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
21	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
22	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
23	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
24	
25	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
26	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
27	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
28	% groups)
29	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
30	% SOURCE QUOTE:
31	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
32	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
33	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
34	% abelian groups defined by the formula
35	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
36	% \end{dfn}
37	%  [\dots]
38	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
39	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
41	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
42	% topologies  by
43	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
44	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
45	%  \end{dfn}"
46	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
47	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
48	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
49	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
50	homomorphisms.}
51	
52	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
53	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
54	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
55	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
56	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
57	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
58	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
59	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
60	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
61	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
62	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
63	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
64	structure. This is the content of
65	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
66	
67	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
68	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
69	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
70	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
71	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
72	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
73	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
74	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
75	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
76	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
77	\begin{enumerate}
78	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
79	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
80	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
81	    isomorphism classes;
82	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
83	    for \(\otimes\);
84	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
85	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
86	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
87	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
88	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
89	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
90	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
91	\end{enumerate}
92	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
93	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
94	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
95	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
96	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
97	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
98	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
99	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
100	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API

1011	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}

1013	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
1014	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
1015	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
1016	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1017	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1018	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
1019	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
1020	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
1021	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
1022	  isomorphism:

1024	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
1025	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}

1032	
1033	\begin{proof}
1034

1037	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
1038	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
1039	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
1040	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
1041	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the

1044	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
