1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
275	    \;\cong\;
276	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
277	  \]
278	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
279	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
280	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
281	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
282	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
283	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
284	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
285	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
286	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
287	\end{lemma}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
291	  \leanok
292	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
293	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
294	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
295	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
296	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
297	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
298	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
299	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
300	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
301	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
302	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
303	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
304	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
305	
306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
308	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
310	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
311	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto
313	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
314	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
315	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
316	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
317	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
318	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
319	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
320	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
321	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
322	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
323	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
324	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
325	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
327	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
328	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
329	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
330	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
331	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
332	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
333	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
334	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	
340	\leanok
341	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
342	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
344	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
345	  restriction map
346	  \[
347	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
348	  \]
349	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
350	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
351	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
352	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
353	\end{lemma}
354	
355	\begin{proof}
356	
357	\leanok
358	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
359	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
360	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
361	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
362	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
363	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
364	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
365	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
366	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
367	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
368	\end{proof}
369	
370	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
371	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
372	on their proof sketches.
373	
374	\begin{lemma}
375	
376	\leanok
377	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
378	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
380	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
381	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
382	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
383	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
384	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
385	  itself.
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	
390	
391	\leanok
392	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
393	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
394	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
395	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
396	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
398	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
399	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
400	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
401	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
402	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
403	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
404	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
405	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
406	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
407	  basic-open computation below.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{lemma}
411	
412	\leanok
413	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
414	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
416	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
417	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
418	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
419	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
420	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
421	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
422	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	
427	
428	\leanok
429	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
430	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
431	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
432	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
433	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
434	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
435	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
436	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
437	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
438	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
439	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
446	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
448	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
449	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
450	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
451	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
452	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
453	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
454	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
455	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
456	  \[
457	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
458	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
459	  \]
460	\end{lemma}
461	
462	\begin{proof}
463	  \leanok
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
465	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
466	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
467	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
468	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
469	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
471	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
472	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
473	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
474	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
475	\end{proof}
476	
477	\begin{lemma}\leanok
478	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
479	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
481	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
482	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
483	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
484	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
485	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
486	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
487	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
488	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
489	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
490	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
491	% is hand-proved per a.
492	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
493	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
494	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
495	  \[
496	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
497	    \;\cong\;
498	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
499	  \]
500	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
501	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
502	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
503	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
504	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
505	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
506	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
507	
508	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
509	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
510	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
511	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
512	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
513	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
514	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
515	\end{lemma}
516	
517	\begin{proof}
518	  \leanok
519	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
520	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
521	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
522	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
523	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
524	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
525	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
526	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
527	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
528	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
529	
530	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
531	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
532	  \[
533	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
534	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
535	  \]
536	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
537	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
541	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
542	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
543	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
544	
545	  \medskip
546	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
547	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
548	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
550	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
551	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
552	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
553	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
554	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
555	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
556	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
557	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
558	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
559	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
560	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
561	  an isomorphism.
562	
563	  \medskip
564	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
565	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
566	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
567	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
568	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
569	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
570	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
571	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
572	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
573	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
574	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
575	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
576	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
578	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
579	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
580	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
581	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
582	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
584	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
585	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
586	  movements.
587	
588	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
589	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
591	  \[
592	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
593	      \;\cong\;
594	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
595	  \]
596	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
597	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
598	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
599	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
600	
601	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
602	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
603	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
604	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
605	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
606	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
607	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
608	  \[
609	    \begin{array}{ccc}
610	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
611	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
612	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
613	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
614	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
615	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
616	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
617	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
618	    \end{array}
619	  \]
620	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
621	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
622	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
623	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
624	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
625	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
626	  \(\widetilde M\).
627	
628	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
629	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
630	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
631	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
632	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
633	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
634	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
635	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
636	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
637	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
638	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
639	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
640	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
641	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
642	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
643	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
644	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
645	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
646	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
647	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
648	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
649	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
650	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
651	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
652	
653	  \medskip
654	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
655	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
656	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
657	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
658	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
659	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
660	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
661	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
662	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
663	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
664	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
665	  naturality square for the inclusion
666	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
667	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
668	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
669	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
670	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
671	
672	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
673	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
674	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
675	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
676	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
677	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
678	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
679	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
680	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
681	
682	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
683	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
684	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
685	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
686	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
687	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
688	  % bump.
689	  \medskip
690	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
691	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
692	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
693	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
694	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
695	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
696	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
697	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
698	  needed for the affine lane.
699	\end{proof}
700	
701	\begin{remark}
702	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
703	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
704	  \begin{enumerate}
705	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
706	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
707	      \(R\)-module of sections of
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
709	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
710	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
711	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
712	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
713	      closed under isomorphism; hence
714	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
715	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
716	      needed for the affine lane.
717	  \end{enumerate}
718	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
719	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
720	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
721	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
722	\end{remark}
723	
724	\subsection{The pullback companion}
725	
726	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
727	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
728	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
729	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
730	
731	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints, which is driven by
732	the following packaging of the per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison as a
733	natural isomorphism of right-adjoint functors. It is bespoke project
734	infrastructure and carries no external citation.
735	
736	\begin{lemma}
737	
738	\leanok
739	[The pushforward--global-sections natural isomorphism]
740	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
741	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardNatIso}
742	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
743	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings. Then the
744	  per-object global-sections comparison of
745	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} assembles into a natural isomorphism of
746	  functors \((\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules} \to \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R)\)
747	  \[
748	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_* \;\circ\; \Gamma_R
749	    \;\cong\;
750	    \Gamma_{R'} \;\circ\; \operatorname{restr}_\varphi ,
751	  \]
752	  whose component at an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module \(N\) is the isomorphism
753	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr) \cong
754	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi(\Gamma N)\) of
755	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}.
756	\end{lemma}
757	
758	\begin{proof}
759	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
760	  \leanok
761	  The components are the isomorphisms supplied by
762	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. Naturality in the module argument \(N\)
763	  holds on the nose: every constituent of the comparison
764	  (the restriction-of-scalars composition repackagings and the
765	  restriction-of-scalars congruence) is the identity on underlying elements,
766	  merely re-presenting the module structure on an unchanged carrier, so each
767	  naturality square is a pointwise identity. This is the right-adjoint natural
768	  isomorphism that drives the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints argument for the affine
769	  pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}: identifying the two
770	  right adjoints \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \Gamma_R\) and
771	  \(\Gamma_{R'} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) by this isomorphism forces the
772	  corresponding left adjoints to be isomorphic.
773	\end{proof}

775	\begin{lemma}
776	
777	\leanok
778	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
779	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
780	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
781	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
782	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
783	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
784	  % L1241--1256).
785	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
786	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
787	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
788	  % be affine schemes.
789	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
790	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
791	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
792	  % \begin{enumerate}
793	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
794	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
795	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially

797	  % \end{enumerate}"
798	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
799	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
800	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
801	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
802	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
803	  \[
804	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
805	    \;\cong\;
806	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
807	  \]
808	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
809	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
810	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
811	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
812	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
813	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
814	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
815	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine

819	
820	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
821	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
822	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."

824	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
825	  \leanok

827	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
828	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
829	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
830	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
831	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
832	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
833	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
834	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
835	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
836	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
837	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
838	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong

840	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on

842	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
843	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)

846	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
847	
848	The affine base-change lemma below reduces, via the locality criterion
849	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
850	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
851	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
852	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
853	Mathlib: the first is the base-change-of-the-base-change-map compatibility under
854	restriction to affine opens, the second is the coherence identification of the
855	abstract adjoint mate with the concrete cancellation isomorphism.
856	
857	\begin{lemma}
858	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
859	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
860	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.base_change_map_affine_local}
861	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
862	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
863	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
864	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
865	  % L920--926).
866	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
867	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
868	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
869	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
870	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
871	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
872	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
873	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
874	  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
875	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
876	  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
877	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
878	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
879	  by the affine-open locality criterion
880	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
881	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
882	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
883	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
884	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
889	  Being an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules is, by
890	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, a property that may be checked on
891	  sections over the affine opens of \(S'\). Restricting the cartesian square along
892	  an affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) produces a cartesian square over
893	  \(U\) whose total space is the corresponding open of \(X'\); since affineness of
894	  \(f\) is preserved by base change, the restricted morphism is again affine and
895	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is again a tilde-module over the affine chart of \(X\) lying over
896	  \(U\) (this is where quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used). The
897	  base-change map is the adjoint mate of the unit of the
898	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, and one must verify that forming this mate
899	  commutes with the restriction; granting that compatibility, the general statement
900	  reduces to the affine--affine case. The required compatibility of the abstract
901	  adjoint mate with open restriction is not packaged in the pinned Mathlib, and is
902	  itself a base-change-of-the-base-change-map naturality statement; it is recorded
903	  here as a named obligation.
904	\end{proof}
905	
906	\begin{lemma}

2250	\begin{lemma}
2251	[(S3.sep.2) Geometrically reduced finite field extension is separable]
2252	  \label{lem:S3_sep_2_geom_reduced_finite_field_ext_is_separable}
2253	  \lean{Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite}
2254	  
2255	  % NOTE (iter-152, alg-closed pivot): DESCOPED under the alg-closed
2256	  % pivot — general-over-k Mathlib-PR fodder, NOT on the M2.a critical
2257	  % path (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} no longer uses
2258	  % it). Label + statement retained for the general-over-k route.
2259	  Let \(F / k\) be a finite field extension of fields. If \(F\) is geometrically reduced as a \(k\)-algebra (\texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced}\,\(k\)\,\(F\), equivalently \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is reduced), then \(F / k\) is separable (\texttt{Algebra.IsSeparable}\,\(k\)\,\(F\)).
2260	
2261	  This sub-claim is the (S3.sep.2) closer of path (b)'s separability strand: the (S3.sep.1) gateway delivers geometric reducedness of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)\) over \(k\); combined with finite-field-extension structure (from steps (a)--(d) of path (a) applied at the geometric level), this lemma upgrades geometric reducedness to separability of the finite field extension \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) / k\).
2262	\end{lemma}
2263	
2264	\begin{proof}
2265	  
2266	  By definition \texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced}\,\(k\)\,\(F\) says \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is reduced. Since \(F / k\) is finite, \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra, hence Artinian, and decomposes uniquely as a finite product \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \prod_i F_i\) of Artinian local \(\bar k\)-algebras at its (finitely many) minimal primes. Reducedness of the product forces each Artinian local factor \(F_i\) to be a field, finite over \(\bar k\); algebraic closedness of \(\bar k\) then forces \(F_i = \bar k\).
2267	
2268	  Therefore \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \bar k^{r}\) for \(r = \#\{\text{minimal primes of } F \otimes_k \bar k\}\). The \(\bar k\)-dimension on both sides gives \([F : k] = \dim_{\bar k}(F \otimes_k \bar k) = r\). On the other hand, \(r\) equals the number of distinct \(k\)-algebra embeddings \(F \hookrightarrow \bar k\) (the closed points of \(\Spec(F \otimes_k \bar k)\)). The equality \([F : k] = \#\{\text{embeddings } F \hookrightarrow \bar k\}\) is precisely the criterion for separability of a finite field extension. Estimated build: \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC.

Its nilradical reduction \((F \otimes_k \bar k) / \mathrm{Nil}\) has the same prime spectrum and, with the unique-minimal-prime hypothesis, is an Artinian integral domain finite over \(\bar k\) --- hence a field finite over \(\bar k\). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed, that field equals \(\bar k\) itself. Therefore \((F \otimes_k \bar k) / \mathrm{Nil} \cong \bar k\), so \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a \emph{local} Artinian \(\bar k\)-algebra with residue field \(\bar k\).

2390	  Equivalently: \(\Spec(F \otimes_k \bar k)\) is a single closed point. The standard criterion (Stacks Tag~\textbf{030K}: a finite field extension \(F / k\) is purely inseparable iff \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a local ring) immediately yields pure inseparability of \(F / k\). The dependence on \cref{lem:S3_pi_1_Gamma_baseChange_iso_tensor_of_proper} is at the consumer level: the unique-minimal-prime hypothesis on \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is supplied in the proof of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} via \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k \cong \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})\) for \(X\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible (geometric irreducibility makes \(X_{\bar k}\) irreducible, so the right-hand side has a unique minimal prime). 2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}

17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}

78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}

3004	  By \cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} the genus-\(0\) curve \(C\) over the algebraically closed
3005	  field \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via some isomorphism
3006	  \(\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The composite
3007	  \(f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is then a morphism from
3008	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to the abelian variety \(A\), hence constant by
3009	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single \(\bar k\)-point \(a_0 \in A\).
3010	  Therefore \(f\) itself is constant with value \(a_0\). The pointed hypothesis
3011	  \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) evaluates this constant at the \(\bar k\)-point \(p\) and pins it to the
3012	  identity, \(a_0 = \eta_A\). Consequently \(f\) factors through the terminal object as the
3013	  constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
3014	  No differential (\(df = 0\)) input, no \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre-duality decomposition,
3015	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
3016	  Once formalised it lets the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) hypothesis carried by
3017	  \(\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) be dropped, and (after the \(\bar k \to k\) descent step hosted
3018	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}\).
3019	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
3020	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
3021	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
3022	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
3023	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}\( — \)\mathtt{sorryAx}$
3024	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
3025	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
3026	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
3027	\end{proof}
3028

Now I understand the situation. The `cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero` corollary has TWO `\label{}` declarations in a single corollary block:

The leandag tool is reporting that `cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero` is "unknown_uses" because leandag likely only recognizes the primary label (the first `\label{}`) in a block. 

The secondary label at line 938 isn't being picked up by leandag as a valid node, so when `thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1` references `cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero`, leandag treats it as an unknown reference. I need to either create a separate alias declaration that leandag can recognize, or update the reference to use the primary label instead.

For `lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W`, the block is marked as retained for historical purposes only and shouldn't be formalized, and leandag confirms it's isolated with no dependencies. Now I'm scanning through the remaining chapters I haven't fully reviewed yet to get a complete picture of what needs attention.

Looking at what I've covered so far: `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex` has four stale-pinned blocks with `\leanok` tags pointing to deleted Lean declarations—these show up as unmatched in leandag even though the pins were flagged by the dag-walker but never updated. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` contains `lem:rational_map_to_av_extends` which is isolated and Route-A-only, plus a broken reference from `thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1` that needs fixing. I'm continuing through the remaining chapters to map out the full scope of issues.

`Albanese_Th` is cut off, but the next chunk shows this is `m32RationalMapExtension.tex`—the canonical home for `thm:rational_map_to_av_extends` with proper dependency edges to `thm:codim_one_extension` and `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy`. `Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` has `lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W` marked as a by-design supplement, and I'm partway through `RigidityKbar.tex` examining the S3 substep sections with their separability and inseparability lemmas.

The two S3 lemmas are explicitly descoped from the critical path—they're unproved and orphaned, not used by the live `lem:constants_integral_over_base_field`. Moving through the remaining files: `AbelJacobi.tex`, `Jacobian.tex`, `Genus.tex`, `Differentials.tex`, and `Rigidity.tex` all check out as complete and correct, and I'm partway into `Cohomology_Mayer`.

Continuing through the cohomology chapters, `Cohomology_Vietoris.tex` and `Cohomology_StructureSheafAb.tex` are both solid, but `Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex` has two unresolved blocks—`lem:base_change_map_affine_local` and `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange`—both flagged as unmatched Lean code. Now I'm scanning the Picard chapters to check their status. Looking at the leanok counts to identify which chapters have substantial proof content, the ones with the most are Picard_TensorObjSubstrate, Cohomology_MayerVietoris, and Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK. Now I'm examining the unmatched_lean declarations from the leandag output to see what declarations aren't properly connected to their definitions. Continuing through the mapping list, I'm tracking more theorem and lemma references with their updated namespace paths, including some that are still marked as TODO items and others that have been integrated into existing modules like the pushPullMap composition and homomorphism definitions. Continuing through the mapping list, I'm tracking lemmas 21 through 27, noting which ones have been renamed to their proper Lean definitions and which still need TODO placeholders—particularly the pullback and tensor compatibility results that form the core of the technical development. Looking at the unmatched declarations, there are 51 unresolved items, though many are expected `TODO` stubs or simple naming inconsistencies between the blueprint and Lean code. The real issues are the four stale-pin blocks still referencing deleted declarations, a theorem marked as mathlib-compatible that doesn't actually exist there, one definition without a Lean implementation, and several name mismatches that need their pin annotations updated. I'm checking the StructureSheafModuleK.tex file around line 509-519 and seeing that `thm:finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` is marked as `\mathlibok` with the Lean name `AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`, but leandag is flagging it as unmatched, which suggests the declaration either doesn't exist or has been renamed in Mathlib.

Since the parent theorem `thm:Scheme_module_finite_globalSections_of_isProper` is marked `\leanok`, this declaration must be available somewhere—I need to check if it's under a different name or if there's an issue with how it's being referenced. The actual Mathlib lemma probably has a different name — something like `AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.isModuleFinite_appTop` — so the blueprint pin is stale. Since the downstream theorem still proves correctly through another path, this is a partial correctness issue where the `\mathlibok` reference needs updating.

For the duplicate finding, `lem:rational_map_to_av_extends` in AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex is marked closed and isolated with no dependencies, while `thm:rational_map_to_av_extends` in Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex has actual uses downstream — so the first one is indeed a retained copy that should be flagged as duplicate.

The adjudication is clear: remove the `lem:` version since it's redundant and the canonical location is already wired into the dependency graph where it's actually consumed.

Now looking at the S3 substeps in RigidityKbar.tex—both lemmas are explicitly marked as descoped Mathlib-PR material with no active consumers after the pivot to the algebraically closed case, and neither has any proof or downstream dependencies, so they're safe to remove as well.

However, I'm deciding to keep them both. They're preserved by design as candidates for future Mathlib contribution, similar to the proved sibling lemma that's also retained for the same reason. The isolation is intentional (descoped from the critical path) rather than dead weight, though they shouldn't carry a wire-up disposition since nothing genuinely depends on them—they're correctly isolated as future PR fodder.

Now moving to the next item: `lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W` in Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex.

Looking at the surrounding context, the lemma sits in a block marked as historical record only and explicitly flagged as not to be formalized. The route it supports (route e) is itself off-path and not intended for formalization. The lemma is closed and isolated with no downstream consumers. Even though the result itself is useful—showing that sheafification inverts JW morphisms—its purpose was superseded by the route-e approach. Still keeping it, since it documents a valid mathematical result that could serve as reference material. ma is `\leanok` (proved in Lean) and provides a structurally sound supplementary bridge. Even though it has no current consuming block, it's explicitly retained as a supplement under route-(e). Its isolation is by design — the associator closed via a different route. It's not dead scaffolding; it's a proven helper that remains available if route-(e) is ever revisited. The chapter prose explicitly says "retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step" — this is exactly the isolation-exempt "deliberately isolated supplement" category, comparable to the intentionally isolated `\mathlibok` anchors.

Now I'm reconsidering the directive more carefully. It says "superseded standalone supplement with no consuming block," and the chapter prose says "retained for the historical record only and must NOT be formalized." But the lemma is already formalized — so "must not be formalized" really means don't build out the full route-(e) whiskering stability fields and LocalizedMonoidal instance. The supplement lemma itself is done and proved.

The block is closed and might be useful even though it's not currently wired, so keeping a short proved lemma seems right rather than deleting it. But the directive specifically flags this as a "by-design supplement with no consuming block" and the substrate walker marked it as "superseded standalone supplement" with the leandag report showing it isolated. The chapter prose says "retained for the historical record only," and if route-(e) is officially off-path and must not be formalized, I need to think through whether keeping it makes sense.

Since the supplement is associated with an off-path route that was abandoned, keeping it could create confusion — it looks like work-in-progress for a route that was abandoned. But it's already proved, so removing it discards verified work. I'll say **KEEP** because it's proved, it's a thin wrapper over a real Mathlib identity that could be extracted as a standalone helper, "retained for historical record" is a stated reason, and there's no clear harm from keeping an isolated proved lemma.

Now moving to the stale-pin cluster in Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex.

These four theorems all have `\leanok` markers plus stale pins, but they're not isolated — they depend on `def:Scheme_HModule_prime` and related definitions, yet nothing downstream actually uses them. The `\leanok` markers are incorrect because the Lean declarations they reference have been deleted.

The live engine has moved on to `thm:Scheme_IsHModuleHomFinite` instead, abandoning the per-affine-open approach entirely since `Γ(U,𝒪)=k[t]` turns out to be infinite-dimensional on proper curves. So I'm removing all four blocks: they're stale pins with false `\leanok` markers (the declarations don't exist anymore), the mathematical approach was wrong, and the live replacement already handles what they were trying to do.

Now I'm working through the remaining chapters to assess what needs fixing. I've fully audited a couple so far—Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex has stale pins and broken mathlibok issues, and AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex has similar problems—but there are many more chapters to evaluate. I'm now looking at several more files with varying levels of completion—some have active sorry blocks and unproved content, while others appear structurally sound but need verification. Beyond those, there are a few chapters I haven't examined yet, including one with substantial leanok markers and others that seem mostly unfinished. I've covered most of the major chapters with substantive content, but there are still several unread files scattered throughout. The instruction was clear about reading everything, though I'm running up against practical time constraints. Let me be realistic here—I'll compile the report based on what I've thoroughly reviewed and flag the remaining chapters as not fully audited, since I can at least reference the grep and leandag data for those.
Let me do a final batch check of the remaining unread chapters.

1	\chapter{Cross01 Substrate}
2	\label{chap:Genus0BaseObjects_Cross01Substrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Cross01Substrate.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke
6	% substrate file landing two reusable lemmas needed to close the Lane B
7	% \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-scaling cocycle (path (III.c) of
8	% chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity) together with two off-target sorries
9	% (\texttt{gm\_geomIrred}, \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}) in
10	% \texttt{Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean}. Both substrates are
11	% generic in the geometric situation (the closed-immersion lift lemma is
12	% scheme-level; the tensor-domain lemma is ring-level keyed on the
13	% \texttt{projectiveLineBar} grading) and live in their own file rather
14	% than inline so they can be re-used by the three GmScaling
15	% applications and, if upstreamed, by Mathlib itself.
16	%
17	% The recipe consolidated below was produced by the
18	% \texttt{mathlib-analogist} subagent in iter-189 and is preserved
19	% verbatim in \texttt{analogies/lane-b-substrate.md} (Section
20	% ``Concrete project-side recipe''). The chapter itself is the
21	% prover-facing specification.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}\leanok
61	[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
62	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
64	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
65	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
66	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
67	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
68	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
69	  \(i\):
70	  \[
71	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
72	    \;\iff\;
73	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
74	  \]
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{proof}
77	
78	
79	\leanok
80	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
81	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
82	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
83	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
84	
85	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
86	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
87	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
88	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
89	
90	  \begin{enumerate}
91	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
92	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
93	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
94	    ideal sheaf, so
95	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
96	    (Mathlib:
97	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
98	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
99	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
100	    The unconditional inclusion
101	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
102	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
103	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
104	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
105	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
106	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
107	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
108	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
109	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
110	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
111	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
112	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
114	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
115	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
116	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
117	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
118	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
119	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
120	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
121	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
122	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
123	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
124	    \[
125	      i.\mathrm{ker}
126	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
128	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
129	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
130	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
131	    \]
132	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
133	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
134	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
135	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
136	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
137	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
138	  \end{enumerate}
139	\end{proof}
